original_hypothesis.title: credMerkleRoot Overwrite When Creating New Art for Same CredId Invalidates Existing Claims
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] is a single shared slot per (chainId, credId) pair. When _initializePhiArt is called for a new art on the same credId, it overwrites the existing merkle root. This invalidates all pending Merkle claims for previously created arts that relied on the old root.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Multiple arts can share the same credId (they use the same NFT contract via credNFTContracts). Each call to createERC1155Internal triggers _initializePhiArt which unconditionally sets credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash. If the new art has a different merkle root, all merkleClaim calls for previous arts will fail proof verification.

proof_of_signal.pattern: credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash unconditionally overwrites on each art creation

preconditions: Two or more arts created for the same credId with MERKLE verification
The new art has a different merkle root than the previous one
Users of the previous art have not yet claimed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: credMerkleRoot is keyed only by (credChainId, credId), not by artId
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Overwrite invalidates proofs for existing arts; merkleClaim reads credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId]
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-art merkle root storage; no check if root is already set

analysis_questions: How often do multiple arts share the same credId?
Can a malicious creator exploit this to selectively deny claims?
Can this be weaponized by anyone who can get a signature for art creation?
validation_requirements: Create artA with credId=1 and rootA, create artB with credId=1 and rootB
Verify merkleClaim for artA with proofs against rootA fails

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Art A created for credId=1 with merkle root R1. 2. Users prepare claims with proofs against R1. 3. Art B created for credId=1 with merkle root R2. 4. credMerkleRoot[chainId][1] = R2. 5. Users trying to claim Art A with proofs against R1 fail.
prerequisites: Ability to create a new art for the same credId (requires signed data from phiSigner)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Legitimate users cannot claim their earned credential NFTs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All pending Merkle claimants for the overwritten credId
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Would require setting credMerkleRoot back; no user self-recovery

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for multiple arts with same credId

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Between art creation and user claims
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Merkle root change visible via credMerkleRoot public mapping

validation_result.impact_summary: Creating a new art for an existing credId unconditionally overwrites the shared credMerkleRoot, invalidating all pending Merkle claims for previously created arts that relied on the old root.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The credMerkleRoot mapping in PhiFactory.sol is keyed by (credChainId, credId), not by artId. The _initializePhiArt function unconditionally sets credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash on every art creation. The system explicitly supports multiple arts for the same credId (evidenced by the _useExistingNFTContract path in createERC1155Internal). When merkleClaim is called, it looks up credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId] - this shared root. If a second art has been created for the same credId with a different merkle root, the old root is gone and proofs against it will fail verification. This is a structural code defect: the storage key doesn't match the intended granularity of the data.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Line 496 of PhiFactory.sol (within _initializePhiArt) shows the unconditional overwrite. The merkleClaim function at ~line 365 reads from the same shared mapping. The createERC1155Internal function explicitly handles existing credNFTContracts (line ~454-457), proving multi-art-per-credId is a supported pattern.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Unconditionally overwrites the merkle root for the (credChainId, credId) pair. All arts sharing this credId use this single root for claim verification. A new art creation replaces the root, breaking proofs for previous arts.
Reads credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId] which is the shared root. If this root was overwritten by a newer art creation for the same credId, proofs valid against the original root will fail.
The else branch (credNFTContracts already set) proves multiple arts per credId is a supported pattern. _initializePhiArt is called before this branch, so the merkle root is already overwritten.

calling_sequence: 1. createArt validates signature, decodes data, calls createERC1155Internal
4. createERC1155Internal checks if credNFTContracts[credChainId][credId] exists, uses existing contract if so
5. Later, merkleClaim for a previous artId reads credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId] (line ~365)
6. MerkleProofLib.verifyCalldata fails because the root no longer matches the proof
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: _initializePhiArt at line 496 writes to credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] unconditionally. This mapping is keyed only by (credChainId, credId), shared across all arts. When merkleClaim at line ~365 reads this mapping using the art's credChainId and credId, it gets the latest root, not the root that was set when the claiming art was created. If a second art for the same credId has been created with a different root, the old proofs become invalid.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this issue. It is a structural code defect in the storage key design.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: 1. Creator (or protocol) creates Art A for credId=1 on chainId=X with merkle root R1. Users receive valid proofs against R1. 2. Creator creates Art B for credId=1 on chainId=X with merkle root R2 (perhaps a different set of eligible users). This is a legitimate operation - the code explicitly supports multiple arts per credId via _useExistingNFTContract. 3. credMerkleRoot[X][1] is now R2, overwriting R1. 4. Users attempting to claim Art A with proofs against R1 get InvalidMerkleProof revert. The attack requires the ability to create art (needs phiSigner signature), but the defect also manifests in legitimate protocol operations where the protocol itself creates multiple arts for the same cred.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Legitimate users who were eligible for Art A under merkle root R1 lose their ability to claim. This is not recoverable by the users themselves. The protocol owner would need to deploy a new root or take corrective action. Additionally, this creates a potential griefing vector: anyone who can obtain a phiSigner signature for art creation on the same credId can invalidate all pending claims for that credId's arts.
recommended_mitigations: Store the merkle root per artId instead of per (credChainId, credId). Change credMerkleRoot to mapping(uint256 artId => bytes32) and update merkleClaim to use arts[artId_].artMerkleRoot instead of the shared mapping.

Alternatively, add a check in _initializePhiArt to skip overwriting if the merkle root is already set and non-zero: if (credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] == bytes32(0)) credMerkleRoot[credChainId][credId] = merkleRootHash;
If the intent is truly one root per credId, add an explicit check that the new root matches the existing one, or revert if different.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy PhiFactory with a test phiSigner. 2. Create Art A for credId=1 with MERKLE verification and merkle root R1 (tree containing address Alice). 3. Generate valid proof for Alice against R1. 4. Create Art B for credId=1 with MERKLE verification and merkle root R2 (tree NOT containing Alice). 5. Attempt merkleClaim for Art A with Alice's proof against R1 - expect InvalidMerkleProof revert. 6. Verify credMerkleRoot[chainId][1] == R2.
uncertainties: Whether phiSigner would in practice sign art creation data with different merkle roots for the same credId - though the code should enforce correctness regardless of off-chain behavior.
The exact frequency of multiple arts sharing the same credId in production use.
additional_testing_needed: Test creating two MERKLE arts for the same credId with different roots and verify the first art's claims fail.
Test creating two MERKLE arts for the same credId with the SAME root and verify both arts' claims succeed.
Test the edge case where Art B has merkleRootHash = bytes32(0) - this would cause all MERKLE claims for that credId to revert with InvalidMerkleProof due to the zero check.
